
Graphics Engine Bible 2026
Volume II — Rendering Systems & Physically Based Rendering

Chapter 2: Deferred Rendering Architecture

=====================================================================
2.1 Introduction
=====================================================================

Deferred rendering is one of the most influential rendering techniques
in modern real-time graphics. Rather than calculating lighting while
drawing geometry, deferred rendering separates geometry processing from
lighting evaluation.

The central idea is simple:

    Geometry Pass -> G-Buffer -> Lighting Pass -> Post Processing

This decoupling allows thousands of dynamic lights to be processed
without repeatedly rendering scene geometry.

Deferred rendering became popular because traditional forward rendering
scales poorly when large numbers of lights are visible simultaneously.

=====================================================================
2.2 Historical Context
=====================================================================

Early engines relied almost entirely on forward rendering.

For every object:

    For every affecting light:
        Render object

This creates a complexity roughly proportional to:

    Objects × Lights

As scene complexity increased, lighting costs became prohibitive.

Deferred rendering changed the relationship by storing surface data
once and performing lighting calculations later in screen space.

This shifted cost toward:

    Visible Pixels × Lights

which is often significantly more efficient.

=====================================================================
2.3 Core Pipeline
=====================================================================

A typical deferred renderer consists of:

1. Depth Prepass
2. Geometry Pass
3. Lighting Pass
4. Transparency Pass
5. Post Processing
6. Presentation

Pipeline overview:

Scene Geometry
      |
      V
+----------------+
| Depth Prepass  |
+----------------+
      |
      V
+----------------+
| G-Buffer Pass  |
+----------------+
      |
      V
+----------------+
| Lighting Pass  |
+----------------+
      |
      V
+----------------+
| Post FX        |
+----------------+
      |
      V
+----------------+
| Present        |
+----------------+

=====================================================================
2.4 Depth Prepass
=====================================================================

Many engines perform a depth-only pass before populating the G-buffer.

Benefits:

- Early Z rejection
- Reduced overdraw
- Better cache behavior
- More stable performance

Only depth values are written.

No lighting calculations occur.

=====================================================================
2.5 G-Buffer Fundamentals
=====================================================================

The G-buffer stores material and geometric information required for
later lighting evaluation.

Common layout:

RT0:
    Albedo.rgb
    Ambient Occlusion

RT1:
    Normal.xyz
    Roughness

RT2:
    Metallic
    Specular
    Material Flags

RT3:
    Emissive
    Custom Data

Depth Buffer:
    Linear or hardware depth

Velocity Buffer:
    Motion vectors

The exact layout varies between engines.

=====================================================================
2.6 Memory Considerations
=====================================================================

Deferred rendering is memory intensive.

Example:

1920 x 1080 resolution

4 Render Targets
32 bits per channel

Memory consumption can easily exceed hundreds of megabytes per frame.

Strategies:

- G-buffer packing
- Reduced precision formats
- MRT optimization
- Bandwidth reduction

Common formats:

RGBA8
RGBA16F
R11G11B10F
RG16F

=====================================================================
2.7 Normal Encoding
=====================================================================

Storing normals efficiently is critical.

Options include:

- Full XYZ storage
- Octahedral encoding
- Spherical mapping
- Two-component reconstruction

Octahedral encoding is widely adopted because it provides excellent
quality while minimizing memory requirements.

Benefits:

- Reduced bandwidth
- Better cache efficiency
- Smaller G-buffer footprint

=====================================================================
2.8 Lighting Pass
=====================================================================

The lighting pass reads G-buffer data and reconstructs surface
properties.

Required inputs:

- Position
- Normal
- Material data
- Light parameters

Per-pixel lighting becomes:

Read G-buffer
Reconstruct position
Evaluate BRDF
Accumulate light contribution

Because geometry is no longer rendered, lighting cost becomes largely
independent of scene complexity.

=====================================================================
2.9 Light Volumes
=====================================================================

Older deferred renderers frequently used light volumes.

Examples:

- Sphere lights
- Cone lights
- Capsule lights

Workflow:

1. Render volume geometry
2. Restrict lighting calculations
3. Accumulate contributions

Advantages:

- Reduced pixel processing
- Better scalability

Disadvantages:

- Additional draw calls
- Volume management complexity

=====================================================================
2.10 Tiled Deferred Rendering
=====================================================================

Modern engines often divide the screen into tiles.

Typical tile size:

16x16 pixels

Workflow:

1. Build light lists
2. Assign lights to tiles
3. Process only relevant lights

Advantages:

- Massive scalability
- Reduced overdraw
- Better GPU utilization

=====================================================================
2.11 Clustered Deferred Rendering
=====================================================================

Clustered rendering extends tiled rendering into depth space.

Instead of:

Tile(X,Y)

We create:

Cluster(X,Y,Z)

Benefits:

- Improved depth precision
- Better volumetric integration
- Smaller light lists

Many modern engines favor clustered approaches.

=====================================================================
2.12 Transparency Challenges
=====================================================================

Deferred rendering struggles with transparency.

Reasons:

- G-buffer stores only one surface
- Multiple layers cannot easily coexist

Common solutions:

- Forward transparency pass
- Weighted blended OIT
- Hybrid rendering

Most production engines use a hybrid architecture.

=====================================================================
2.13 Shadows Integration
=====================================================================

Shadow maps integrate naturally into deferred pipelines.

Lighting stage:

Sample shadow map
Evaluate visibility
Apply attenuation

Supported techniques:

- PCF
- VSM
- EVSM
- MSM
- Ray-traced shadows

=====================================================================
2.14 Screen Space Effects
=====================================================================

Deferred rendering provides data useful for screen-space techniques.

Examples:

SSAO
HBAO
GTAO
SSR
SSGI

These effects rely heavily on:

- Depth
- Normals
- Motion vectors

all readily available from the G-buffer.

=====================================================================
2.15 Performance Analysis
=====================================================================

Advantages:

- Excellent many-light performance
- Efficient BRDF evaluation
- Flexible material systems
- Rich post-processing support

Disadvantages:

- High bandwidth usage
- Large memory footprint
- Transparency complications
- MSAA challenges

=====================================================================
2.16 Production Recommendations
=====================================================================

For modern engines:

Use clustered lighting.
Minimize G-buffer size.
Compress normals.
Use temporal reconstruction.
Profile memory bandwidth continuously.
Separate transparency into forward passes.

Deferred rendering remains one of the most practical and scalable
architectures for real-time graphics, forming the foundation of many
AAA rendering pipelines.

End of Chapter 2.
